segments.title: Wikipedia as a research starting point
segments.summary: Historians may use Wikipedia to orient themselves and follow its source list, but not as the final authority for a claim.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke rejects the idea that historians never consult Wikipedia. He describes it as a quick way to get bearings on an unfamiliar subject and, especially, to locate a page's cited works. The useful research habit is to follow those citations outward, then consult their bibliographies and sources in turn. That creates a traceable chain of evidence rather than relying on a single mutable summary. He also notes that its images can be useful for explanatory video work.

evidence.note: The transcript explains using Wikipedia to find sources and bibliographies.

segments.title: Why Wikipedia is not stable citable evidence
segments.summary: The problem with citing Wikipedia is accountability and stability: entries can change and do not provide one accountable author.
segments.body: A citation should let another reader return to the same evidence and assess the person or work responsible for it. Dr. Clarke argues that a Wikipedia entry lacks a single author who can be held accountable for an error. Its editable nature is also a double-edged feature: it can be current, but it can also be altered by bad actors or change after a researcher has read it. That makes it difficult to cite as a fixed source. His point connects historical method to reproducibility, since another scholar should be able to retrace the research and reach a comparable result.

evidence.note: The transcript cites mutable entries and absent individual accountability as barriers to citation.

segments.title: Citation styles and the flow of an argument
segments.summary: Harvard, Chicago, and Vancouver all identify sources, but footnotes or endnotes can keep citations from interrupting historical prose.
segments.body: The video compares Harvard's parenthetical in-text citations with Chicago-style footnotes and Vancouver-style endnotes. Dr. Clarke sees the systems as serving the same basic purpose while placing the reference in different locations. He argues that historians often prefer notes because repeated brackets can break the flow of an analytical case. Footnotes keep a source readily available to readers, while endnotes can be easier for publishers to place in print. The choice is therefore partly about scholarly transparency and partly about the readability and production of an argument.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Harvard, Chicago, and Vancouver and explains note placement.
